exercise.puppy: Puppies (under 1 year) need 5 minutes of exercise per month of age, twice a day. Over-exercising can damage developing joints.
exercise.adult: Adult dogs need at least 30-60 minutes of exercise daily. High-energy breeds like Huskies and Border Collies need 2+ hours.
exercise.senior: Senior dogs (7+ years) still need daily walks but shorter ones — 20-30 minutes at a gentle pace. Watch for signs of fatigue or joint pain.

feeding.puppy: Puppies should be fed 3-4 times a day using age-appropriate food. Avoid adult food — it lacks the nutrients growing dogs need.
feeding.adult: Adult dogs should be fed twice a day at consistent times. Avoid feeding immediately before or after vigorous exercise to reduce bloat risk.
feeding.senior: Senior dogs may need fewer calories but more joint-supporting nutrients. Split meals into smaller, more frequent portions if digestion is a concern.

grooming.general: Most dogs need brushing 2-3 times a week. Long-haired breeds need daily brushing. Bathe every 4-6 weeks unless the dog gets dirty.
grooming.nails: Trim nails every 3-4 weeks. Overgrown nails cause pain and affect posture.

health.general: Dogs should have an annual vet check-up. Keep vaccinations, flea, tick, and heartworm treatments up to date.
health.medications: Administer medications at the same time each day with or without food as directed. Never skip doses for antibiotics or heartworm prevention.
health.hydration: Dogs need approximately 30ml of water per kg of body weight daily. Always provide fresh water, especially after exercise.

enrichment.general: Mental stimulation is as important as physical exercise. Puzzle feeders, scent games, and training sessions prevent boredom and destructive behaviour.
enrichment.social: Dogs are social animals. Aim for daily interaction, play, and if possible, socialization with other dogs.

exercise.kitten: Kittens are naturally active and need several short play sessions a day — at least 2-3 sessions of 10-15 minutes using interactive toys.
exercise.adult: Adult cats need 20-30 minutes of active play daily. Use wand toys or laser pointers to simulate hunting behaviour.
exercise.senior: Senior cats (10+ years) are less active but still need gentle play to maintain muscle tone and mental health.

feeding.general: Cats should be fed twice a day at consistent times. Avoid free-feeding dry food as it contributes to obesity.
feeding.hydration: Cats have a low thirst drive and are prone to dehydration. Wet food provides extra moisture. Always keep fresh water available — a fountain encourages drinking.
feeding.kitten: Kittens need calorie-dense kitten food fed 3-4 times daily to support rapid growth.

grooming.short_hair: Short-haired cats groom themselves but benefit from brushing once a week to reduce hairballs.
grooming.long_hair: Long-haired cats need daily brushing to prevent mats and tangles. Check behind the ears and under the legs.
grooming.general: Trim nails every 2-3 weeks. Check ears weekly for wax build-up or signs of infection.

health.general: Cats need annual vet check-ups. Keep vaccinations and flea treatments current.
health.medications: For cats with hyperthyroidism or other chronic conditions, medication must be given at the same time daily. Use a pill pocket or mix with a small amount of wet food if the cat resists.
health.dental: Dental disease is common in cats. Brushing teeth 2-3 times a week or providing dental treats reduces tartar build-up.

enrichment.general: Cats need vertical space (cat trees, shelves) and hiding spots to feel secure. Rotate toys weekly to maintain novelty.
enrichment.indoor: Indoor cats need more deliberate enrichment — window perches, bird feeders outside windows, and puzzle feeders prevent boredom.

exercise.general: Rabbits need at least 3-4 hours of free-roaming exercise outside their enclosure daily. A cramped cage without exercise time causes physical and psychological harm.
exercise.adult: Adult rabbits are most active at dawn and dusk. Schedule exercise and play sessions around these natural activity windows.

feeding.general: 80% of a rabbit's diet should be fresh hay (timothy or orchard grass). Fresh leafy greens daily and a small amount of pellets. Avoid sugary treats and fruit — these cause gut problems.
feeding.hydration: Fresh water must be available at all times. A bowl is preferable to a bottle as it is more natural and easier to drink from.
feeding.warning: Never feed iceberg lettuce, potatoes, or anything from the onion family — these are toxic to rabbits.

grooming.general: Brush rabbits 2-3 times a week; daily during moulting season. Rabbits groom themselves but cannot vomit hairballs — ingested fur causes dangerous blockages.
grooming.nails: Trim nails every 6-8 weeks. Overgrown nails curl and cause pain when walking.

health.general: Rabbits hide illness well. A sudden change in eating, droppings, or behaviour is a red flag — contact a vet promptly.
health.medications: Rabbits are sensitive to many antibiotics. Never give a rabbit medication prescribed for another species. Administer rabbit-safe medications at consistent times.
health.gi_stasis: GI stasis (gut slowdown) is life-threatening. If a rabbit stops eating or producing droppings for more than 12 hours, seek emergency vet care immediately.

enrichment.general: Rabbits are intelligent and social. Provide tunnels, cardboard boxes, and chew toys. Bored rabbits become destructive and depressed.
enrichment.social: Rabbits are happiest in bonded pairs. A solo rabbit needs significantly more human interaction and enrichment time to stay mentally healthy.

general.general: Ensure your pet has fresh water, appropriate food for its species, and daily interaction. Research species-specific needs — care requirements vary widely.
general.health: Schedule annual vet check-ups. Keep a log of any changes in behaviour, appetite, or activity level to share with your vet.
general.enrichment: All pets benefit from environmental enrichment suited to their natural behaviours. Provide safe opportunities to explore, forage, or play.
